title: Multi-Horizon Pit-Imminence Model
summary: Discrete-time survival work shows time-to-event problems often improve when framed as several linked binary hazards over future intervals.
rationale: Discrete-time survival work shows time-to-event problems often improve when framed as several linked binary hazards over future intervals. For pit strategy, “next lap” is noisy; jointly learning pit-in-1, pit-in-2, and pit-in-3-laps can stabilize the true pit-window signal.
implementation_hint: From each driver-race sequence, derive auxiliary labels for pit within <=1, <=2, <=3 laps. Train a shared-head CatBoost/XGB stack or small neural multitask model, then use the 1-lap head directly or blend it with finite differences between horizons to sharpen ranking.
expected_effect: Better separation near the pit window and a new OOF signal that is correlated but not identical to the current binary target.
risk: Sequence labeling must be done strictly within each driver-race trajectory; naive construction can leak across folds or create inconsistent labels around session ends.
